fact: February 29 only exists every 4 years, which means if you were born on this day, you'd only have a real birthday once every four years!
In the Northern Hemisphere, February 29 falls right when winter is getting tired and spring is starting to wake up - you might see the first brave flowers poking through snow!
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier and sets about 2 minutes later on February 29 compared to February 28, giving you an extra 4 minutes of daylight to play outside.
February 29 is exactly one month before the spring equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal lengths all around the world.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, February 29 happens right at the end of summer when leaves are turning colors and falling - like their version of October!
Groundhogs are still hibernating on February 29, sleeping so deeply underground that they won't wake up for another few weeks.
This date falls smack in the middle of Pisces season, making it one of the most watery and dreamy days of the zodiac calendar.
In cold places like Canada and Alaska, February 29 is when ice on lakes and rivers starts making loud cracking sounds as it begins to shift and melt.
Tree sap starts flowing faster around February 29 in maple trees, which is why maple syrup makers get busy tapping trees right around this time.
The moon looks exactly the same on February 29 as it would on February 28 in non-leap years, but you get an extra day to stare at it and wonder about space.
In tropical places near the equator, February 29 marks the start of the dry season when rain becomes rare and animals start gathering around water holes.
Snow crystals formed on February 29 are more likely to be the chunky, wet kind that's perfect for snowball fights instead of the powdery stuff from January.
